hook: Roman Road's marble-counter eel and pie shop, in the Kelly family since the 1930s
facts: Trading as a pie and mash shop on Roman Road since the early 1920s; George Kelly took it on in 1939.
Run today by Sue, a Kelly descendant, and family, still mincing beef and baking on the premises.
Inspired Melanie McGrath's book 'Pie and Mash Down the Roman Road'.
w: Pie, mash and a slick of parsley liquor, served at marble counters on Roman Road since the 1920s; George Kelly took the lease in 1939 and four generations have minced the beef and baked out the back ever since. Melanie McGrath wrote a whole book about it. Eels jellied, tea stewed, tradition unbudged.

hook: Vintage eel-and-pie house on Bethnal Green Road, once the Krays' regular
facts: Pie and mash counter at 414 Bethnal Green Road, run by the Kelly family for around a century.
Long associated with the Kray twins, who grew up nearby on Vallance Road.
Still serves pie, mash, liquor and jellied eels at tiled benches; for years traded as S&R Kelly.
legend: Local lore holds the Krays took their pie and mash here as boys.
w: Hot pies, mash and liquor doled out across tiled benches at 414 Bethnal Green Road for the best part of a century, long known to regulars as S&R Kelly. The Krays grew up round the corner on Vallance Road; legend has it they took their pie and mash here as boys. Jellied eels, stewed tea, no airs.

hook: Chrisp Street Market's tiled pie-and-mash bar, more than half a century deep
facts: Trading on the pedestrianised Chrisp Street Market in Poplar for over 50 years.
Maureen's son Jason runs it now with his wife Karen; beef minced on the premises.
Serves pie, mash and liquor, jellied or stewed eels, and salt beef beigels.
w: Looks like a tiled greasy spoon, cooks like an East End institution. Maureen's has held its pitch on pedestrianised Chrisp Street Market for over fifty years, the beef minced on site, the dark liquor almost minty. Son Jason runs it now with wife Karen. Pie, mash, jellied eels and a salt-beef beigel on the side.

hook: Camden's 1934 pie shop, decor unchanged for the best part of a century
facts: Opened on Royal College Street in 1934 and still family-run.
Interior little altered in decades; original tiling, mirrors and benches survive.
Serves eels, meat pies and mash plus old-school puddings like spotted dick.
w: A time capsule on Royal College Street, open since 1934 and barely touched since: original tiling, foxed mirrors, hard benches. Eels, meat pies and a scrape of mash, finished with proper old puds, jam sponge and spotted dick. One of north London's last survivors of the trade. Liquor green, decor frozen.

hook: Portobello Road's pie-and-mash holdout under the shadow of Trellick Tower
facts: At the north end of Portobello Road, near Trellick Tower; established 1995.
One of only a handful of pie and mash shops surviving in west London.
Suet-bottomed pies and buttery crusts served in heavy white china bowls.
w: West London's pie and mash all but vanished; Cockneys, at the rough top end of Portobello Road under Trellick Tower, keeps the tradition alive. Suet-bottomed pies, buttery crusts, mash and liquor heaped into heavy white china. A relative youngster, opened 1995, but proper to the bone. Eels jellied, market roaring outside.

hook: Tooting's eel-and-pie shop, the menu unchanged since 1908
facts: Trading on Selkirk Road, Tooting since 1908; the menu reportedly unchanged since.
Serves mash, jellied and stewed eels and 'licker', the parsley liquor.
Owners announced in 2024 the family would sell up; still trading meanwhile.
w: Since 1908 the menu at Harringtons on Selkirk Road has barely moved: pie, mash, 'licker' green with parsley, eels jellied or stewed. A south London survivor where the tiles gleam and the regulars are lifers. The family announced in 2024 they would sell up; catch it while the ovens are still lit. Time, ticking.

hook: Gray's Inn Road fry-up institution, Italian-run since the war
facts: On Gray's Inn Road since the Second World War, originally across the road and Italian-run.
Renowned for full fry-ups with proper fried slice, plus home-cooked British lunches.
Serves roasts, shepherd's pie, liver and bacon and stewed steak to a loyal trade.
w: An Italian family fry-up on Gray's Inn Road since the war years, when it stood across the street feeding cabbies, clerks and MoD types. Andrew's still does a stupendous full English with proper fried slice, then home-cooked lunches, shepherd's pie, liver and bacon, stewed steak. Formica, banter and gravy. No reinvention required.

hook: Soho's 1933 Italian caff, fry-ups by day and a gin den by night
facts: Opened in 1933 by Ambrose 'Pop', a southern-Italian emigre, as a tea shop and confectioners.
Tucked on Great Chapel Street, one of Soho's oldest surviving caffs.
By night the room becomes The London Gin Club, pouring rare gins from the cafe tables.
w: Opened in 1933 by 'Pop' Ambrose, fresh off the boat from southern Italy, the Star has fed Soho fry-ups and milky tea from a Great Chapel Street side-room ever since. After dark the same tables turn into The London Gin Club, pouring rare bottles where bacon sandwiches sat at noon. Two lives, one room. Old Soho, hanging on.

hook: Old Compton Street's 1960s Italian bar where the prices nearly stood still
facts: Long known simply as Pollo, a Soho budget-Italian institution on Old Compton Street.
Preserves 1960s fittings: ox-blood booths, period light fittings and murals.
Cheap pasta and Italian plates have drawn students and night owls for decades.
w: Generations of skint students and Soho stragglers have squeezed into the ox-blood booths of Pollo on Old Compton Street for vast plates of cheap pasta. The 1960s fittings, murals and lamps survive intact; for years the prices barely moved either. Trading now as La Porchetta Pollo Bar. Carbs, banter, bargain. Soho's stubborn heart.

hook: Borough Market's family greasy spoon, frying since the early 1960s
facts: Maria Moruzzi's parents opened the original Borough Cafe on Park Street in 1961.
Now on Southwark Street, a tiled caff hemmed in by Borough Market's stalls.
Traditional fry-ups and British plates among London's fanciest food market.
w: While Borough Market gentrified into a foodie cathedral, Maria's kept frying eggs and bacon at caff prices. The Moruzzi family opened up on Park Street in 1961 and Maria's daughter carries it on by Southwark Street, tiled and steamy amid the artisan stalls. A bacon roll and a mug of tea, held firm against the tasting menus.

hook: Three-generation greasy spoon facing Putney Bridge station, seascape murals and all
facts: Family-run for three generations at 1A Station Approach, opposite Putney Bridge tube.
Decor unchanged in decades: blue-and-white tiling, plywood panelling, Formica tops.
Hand-painted seascape murals line the walls of the all-day breakfast room.
w: Not the Rogers-designed Italian one upriver; this River Cafe sits squat opposite Putney Bridge station, family-run across three generations. Inside, decades unchanged: blue-and-white tiling, plywood panelling, Formica, hand-painted seascape murals over the fry-ups. A greasy spoon hiding in plain sight. Tea, two sugars.

hook: Holborn's beef-dripping chippy, Formica unchanged since the early sixties
facts: Frying on Theobald's Road since around 1962, still in beef dripping.
Eat-in room of Formica tables and fixed fittings, little altered in sixty years.
A favourite of cabbies, lawyers and night workers in central London.
w: Cod and chips fried the old way in beef dripping, eaten in at Formica tables that have not budged since around 1962. The Fryer's Delight feeds Holborn cabbies, lawyers and shift-workers from a room where the fixtures are, mercifully, fixed. No batter trends, no small plates. Just golden fish, mushy peas and a steaming pot of tea.

hook: Old Roman Road sandwich-bar caff with marble tables and bentwood chairs
facts: Historic Italian sandwich bar on Roman Road, family-run for generations.
Relocated in 2008 after the original premises were struck by a lorry, saving the original marble tables and Thonet chairs.
Preserves an East End that now survives mostly in memory.
w: A Roman Road sandwich bar from the old East End, run by the same family for generations. When a lorry ploughed into the original premises in 2008 they salvaged what mattered, the marble tables and bentwood Thonet chairs, and carried them up the street. Filled rolls, frothy coffee, a vanished London preserved in furniture. Quietly defiant.

hook: Hampstead's coffee lounge since 1954, wood-panelled and stubbornly intact
facts: Open on Hampstead High Street since 1954, billed as one of London's oldest coffee lounges.
Wood-panelled interior and pavement tables little changed over the decades.
A long-standing Hampstead haunt for villagers, writers and regulars.
w: Pouring coffee on Hampstead High Street since 1954, the Coffee Cup claims a spot among London's oldest coffee lounges. Dark wood panelling, pavement seats and a menu running from frothy cappuccino to a full plate; it has watched the village shift for seventy years. Locals, writers and loiterers. Wonderfully unbothered.
